In contrast, P2PKH and P2WPKH addresses remain secure until their first spend reveals the underlying public key. This distinction is critical for migration planning, as only early P2PK coins and addresses that have already transacted are currently at risk.

threat_description: Ethereum relies on ECDSA secp256k1 for transaction signing, exposing the public key in the signature whenever an account broadcasts its first outbound transaction. This revelation creates direct Shor-attack vulnerability for any address that has ever transacted, as the private key can be derived from the exposed public key. While the Ethereum Foundation explores account abstraction via EIP-4337 and EIP-7560 as a path toward quantum-safe wallets, no finalized EIP currently mandates a specific post-quantum migration timeline.
crypto_at_risk: ECDSA secp256k1 transaction signing, public-key exposure on first outbound transaction, BIP-32/39/44 HD wallet derivation
pqc_replacement: Account Abstraction (EIP-4337), Verkle Trees, quantum-resistant signature schemes

threat_description: OpenSSH 9.0 (April 2022) defaulted to sntrup761x25519-sha512, utilizing NTRU Prime rather than ML-KEM for hybrid key exchange. threat_description: Hash function collision resistance halved by quantum algorithms: Grover's algorithm reduces the pre-image resistance of SHA-256 from 256-bit to ~128-bit effective security. Quantum collision attacks (Brassard-Høyer-Tapp / BHT) reduce SHA-256 collision resistance from 128-bit to ~85-bit effective security. SHA-1 (already classically broken) is fully negligible post-CRQC. Quantum security of a hash for collision/preimage is determined by output size: SHA-384, SHA-512, SHA3-384, and SHA3-512 (larger outputs) provide substantially improved quantum margins; SHA3-256 has the same 256-bit output as SHA-256 and therefore equivalent quantum resistance. HMAC-SHA-256 is less affected due to keying.
crypto_at_risk: SHA-1 (broken classically), SHA-256 collision resistance, MD5, RIPEMD-160, Merkle trees using SHA-256
pqc_replacement: SHA-384, SHA-512, SHA3-256, SHA3-512 for collision-sensitive applications; SLH-DSA hash chains use SHA-256/SHA-512 by design
main_source: NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev 6 / NIST IR 8545
